startScreenOptions.title: AN54.1-AN54.4 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 4 embedded questions for AN54.1-AN54.4.

interactions.label: On an erect abdominal radiograph, a crescent of free gas beneath the right hemid

params.question: On an erect abdominal radiograph, a crescent of free gas beneath the right hemidiaphragm is clearly visible. This finding is detectable on a plain film because:

answers.text: Gas and the diaphragm have markedly different X-ray absorption, creating a visible density contrast

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! Gas absorbs almost no X-rays (appears black), while the diaphragm is soft-tissue density (grey). This density difference creates the visible crescent — a direct application of the principle that differential X-ray absorption produces the radiographic image.

answers.text: Intravenous contrast agent was administered before the film was taken

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Subdiaphragmatic free air is detected on a plain radiograph without any contrast agent. The natural density difference between gas and soft tissue is sufficient to produce the finding.

answers.text: Gas absorbs X-rays more strongly than bone, making it appear white on the film

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Gas absorbs X-rays the least of all five radiographic densities and therefore appears black (radiolucent). Bone absorbs X-rays most strongly and appears white.

answers.text: Free intraperitoneal gas emits its own radiation that the detector captures

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Gas does not emit radiation. A radiograph is produced by an external X-ray beam passing through the body; tissues that absorb more of the beam appear brighter on the film.

overallFeedback.feedback: Free gas under the diaphragm is visible because gas (black, radiolucent) and the diaphragm (grey, soft-tissue density) have very different X-ray absorption. This differential absorption is the fundamental principle of plain radiography.
Excellent! Understanding differential absorption across the five radiographic densities — metal/bone, soft tissue, fat, and air — is the foundation of all plain-film interpretation.

interactions.label: While systematically reviewing a plain abdominal radiograph, you find that the l

params.question: While systematically reviewing a plain abdominal radiograph, you find that the left psoas shadow is obliterated while the right side is clearly visible. What does the unilateral loss of the psoas shadow most likely indicate?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The psoas shadow should be visible bilaterally on a normal abdominal radiograph. Its unilateral absence is an abnormal finding warranting further investigation.

answers.text: Retroperitoneal pathology such as an abscess, haematoma, or tumour

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! The psoas major muscle is visible because of the surrounding retroperitoneal fat. When pathology — an abscess, haematoma, or tumour — replaces that fat, the density difference is lost and the shadow disappears on the affected side.

answers.text: Small bowel obstruction on the left side

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Small bowel obstruction presents with centrally dilated loops and air-fluid levels on an erect film, not with psoas shadow obliteration.
Incorrect. Pneumoperitoneum produces free gas under the diaphragm on an erect film, or Rigler's sign (gas outlining both bowel walls) on a supine film — not psoas shadow loss.

overallFeedback.feedback: The psoas shadow is visible due to surrounding retroperitoneal fat. Unilateral obliteration indicates that retroperitoneal pathology — an abscess, haematoma, or tumour — has replaced the fat plane around the muscle.
Well done! Assessing the psoas shadows is the third step of the systematic six-step approach to abdominal radiograph interpretation.

interactions.label: On a barium swallow examination, which characteristic radiological appearance in

params.question: On a barium swallow examination, which characteristic radiological appearance indicates achalasia of the cardia?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The apple-core (napkin-ring) lesion is characteristic of colonic carcinoma on a barium enema, not an oesophageal finding.
Incorrect. The rat-tail pattern is the characteristic irregular narrowing seen in oesophageal carcinoma, not achalasia.
Correct! Achalasia produces a smooth, tapered narrowing at the distal oesophagus described as a bird-beak appearance, caused by failure of the lower oesophageal sphincter to relax.
Incorrect. The cloverleaf deformity is seen in the duodenal cap on a barium meal, indicating chronic duodenal ulcer disease with scarring.

overallFeedback.feedback: Achalasia produces a bird-beak narrowing on barium swallow. Remember the key associations: rat-tail = oesophageal carcinoma, apple-core = colonic carcinoma (barium enema), cloverleaf = chronic duodenal ulcer (barium meal).
Correct! The bird-beak narrowing of the distal oesophagus on barium swallow is a classic finding of achalasia cardia.

interactions.label: Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) has completely replaced ERCP,

params.question: Magnetic Resonance Cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) has completely replaced ERCP, making ERCP obsolete for both diagnostic and therapeutic purposes.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: MRCP has replaced ERCP only for diagnostic imaging. ERCP remains indispensable for therapeutic biliary procedures — sphincterotomy, common bile duct stone extraction, stent placement, and ampullary biopsy. It is not obsolete.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Well done! While MRCP has replaced ERCP for diagnostic imaging of the biliary and pancreatic ducts, ERCP remains the gold standard for therapeutic interventions — sphincterotomy, common bile duct stone extraction, stent placement, and ampullary biopsy.
